actor: Screen Actors Guild – American Federation of Television and Radio Artists (SAG-AFTRA)
description: After a 118-day strike, SAG-AFTRA secured groundbreaking AI protections in its TV/film contract: studios must get consent and pay actors before creating AI replicas of their likeness, and background actors cannot be scanned without compensation. The deal set the standard for AI protections in labor agreements.

The WGA's new contract with studios prohibits AI from writing or rewriting literary material, and bans using AI-generated content as source material that could undermine writers' credits. Writers can choose to use AI tools, but studios cannot require them to. The WGA deal was the first major creative union to win explicit AI prohibitions.

actor: International Alliance of Theatrical Stage Employees (IATSE)
description: IATSE secured AI protections in its TV/theatrical agreement: productions must notify the union before deploying AI tools, workers retain rights over their work, and AI cannot be used to eliminate covered jobs without bargaining.

The Teamsters secured language in UPS and freight contracts requiring the company to bargain before deploying any AI or automation technology that affects bargaining unit jobs, and banning the use of AI in discipline and performance monitoring without human review.

CWA launched the 'No AI Without Labor Protections' campaign, securing contract language at multiple companies — including some major call centers — requiring advance notice and bargaining before AI deployment, plus retraining guarantees for displaced workers.

The NYT updated its terms of service to explicitly prohibit AI companies from scraping its content to train models. It later filed suit against OpenAI and Microsoft. The Times also barred staff from using AI tools for news reporting without editorial oversight.

The AP released a policy prohibiting its journalists from using AI-generated text in news content without disclosure and editorial review. It also negotiated a paid licensing deal with OpenAI — one of the first news organizations to do so, setting a precedent for fair compensation rather than scraping.

Getty Images banned AI-generated images from its platform and filed suit against Stability AI for training on its licensed photos without permission. Getty argued that compensation and consent — not a blanket ban on AI — is the correct model.

Newgrounds, the independent animation and art platform with over 25 years of history, banned AI-generated content from its site entirely. The platform cited the harm to human artists whose work was used without consent to train the models.

Following mass protests from artists on its platform ('No AI Art' campaign), ArtStation introduced an AI-generated art filter allowing artists to opt out of having their work shown alongside AI content. The platform also added a no-AI disclosure tag.

The EU AI Act — the world's first comprehensive AI law — was passed by the European Parliament. It bans real-time biometric surveillance in public, prohibits AI social scoring, requires transparency for AI-generated content, and mandates copyright disclosure for training data. High-risk AI in employment, education, healthcare, and critical infrastructure faces strict requirements.

Governor Newsom signed a package of 18 AI bills, including AB 2602 (requiring consent before using an AI replica of a deceased or living person's voice or likeness), AB 2839 (restricting AI-generated election content), and several laws requiring disclosure of AI in ads and automated decision-making. He vetoed SB 1047, a broad AI safety bill, but signed narrower protections.

Illinois passed the Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act, requiring employers to notify applicants when AI is used to analyze video interviews, explain how the AI works, and obtain consent. Employers must also collect demographic data to audit for bias.

NYC Local Law 144 requires employers using AI in hiring decisions to conduct annual bias audits by independent parties, and to publicly post a summary of the audit results. Employers must also notify candidates when automated employment decision tools are being used.

Colorado passed SB 205, the first US state law requiring companies that deploy high-risk AI to conduct impact assessments, disclose AI use to consumers, and provide a way to appeal automated decisions. The law targets AI used in employment, education, financial services, healthcare, and housing.

Multiple Texas school districts banned AI writing tools including ChatGPT from classrooms, citing academic integrity concerns. Some districts went further and blocked AI tools on school networks entirely while developing AI policies.

After a historic strike against the Big Three automakers, UAW secured contract language requiring advance notice and bargaining before any automation or AI deployment affecting workers, retraining rights for displaced workers, and enhanced severance if jobs are eliminated due to AI.

Thom Yorke and other prominent musicians publicly refused to allow their music to be licensed for AI training, and Radiohead blocked its catalog from AI training datasets. Yorke was among the signatories of an open letter urging regulation of AI music generators.

After NEDA announced it was replacing its human helpline staff with an AI chatbot named Tessa, laid-off workers and advocates launched a public campaign exposing that Tessa had already given harmful advice to people with eating disorders. The campaign led to NEDA suspending the chatbot and sparked national debate about AI replacing mental health workers.

UK media union Prospect negotiated AI clauses into contracts at several major publishers, requiring consultation before AI deployment, prohibiting AI-generated content from replacing union-covered work, and ensuring journalists retain authorship rights over AI-assisted pieces.

actor: Spawning AI / Have I Been Trained
description: Spawning AI launched 'Have I Been Trained' — a tool allowing artists to check whether their work is in AI training datasets like LAION-5B and submit opt-out requests. Over 80 million images have been opted out. Major AI companies including Stability AI agreed to honor the opt-out list.

actor: No AI Fraud Act (US Congress, proposed)
description: Bipartisan legislation introduced in Congress to establish a federal right protecting individuals from unauthorized AI replication of their voice or likeness. Supported by SAG-AFTRA, the Recording Industry Association of America, and major entertainment unions. As of 2025, it had not yet passed.

Proposed federal legislation requiring that AI-generated content — images, videos, audio, and text — be clearly labeled as such. The bill has bipartisan support but as of 2025 has not been passed into law.

Germany's top labor court ruled that works councils (employee representative bodies) must be consulted before employers can deploy AI systems that affect working conditions. The ruling means German workers have a legal right to weigh in on AI adoption in their workplace — a model other EU countries are watching.

Adobe launched a $1 million fund to compensate stock photo contributors whose work was used to train its Firefly AI model, becoming one of the first AI companies to create a concrete revenue-sharing program for human creators. Adobe Firefly was trained only on licensed, public domain, and Adobe Stock content.

Shutterstock launched a 'Contributor Fund' to compensate photographers and artists whose work is used to train AI models — distributing payment based on contribution volume and downloads. The fund was funded partly by Shutterstock's licensing deal with OpenAI for DALL-E training data.
